1.description: Investors' weights. These panels plot the weightsht(θ)for each distinct θ-type investor for different levels of current belief dispersionvt. The belief dispersion isvt=3.23%in solid lines and 3.61% in dashed lines. The vertical dotted lines correspond to the benchmark economy with no belief dispersion. The cash-flow news is relatively badDt=1.22in Panel A and goodDt=2.50in Panel B. The baseline parameter values follow from TableBIof AppendixB, and are based on matching the model-implied belief dispersion moments to the corresponding summary statistics in Yu (2011):m∼=0,v∼=3.39%,μ=14.23%,σ=8.25%,γ=2,t=4.37, andT=10.

2.description: Stock price convexity and effects of belief dispersion. This figure plots the equilibrium stock priceStagainst cash-flow news for different levels of current belief dispersionvt. The dotted line corresponds to the equilibrium stock price in the benchmark economy with no belief dispersion. The baseline parameter values are as in Figure1.

3.description: Effects of risk aversion on stock price. These figures plot the equilibrium stock priceStagainst relative risk aversion coefficient γ for different levels of standard deviation of investor typesv∼. The dotted lines correspond to the equilibrium stock price in the benchmark economy with no belief dispersion. The cash-flow news is relatively badDt=1.22in Panel A and goodDt=2.50in Panel B. The baseline parameter values are as in Figure1.

4.description: Effects of belief dispersion on mean return. This figure plots the equilibrium mean returnμStagainst cash-flow news for different levels of current belief dispersionvt. The dotted line corresponds to the equilibrium mean return in the benchmark economy with no belief dispersion. The baseline parameter values are as in Figure1.

5.description: Effects of belief dispersion on stock volatility. This figure plots the equilibrium stock volatilityσStagainst current belief dispersionvt. The dotted line corresponds to the equilibrium stock volatility in the benchmark economy with no belief dispersion. The baseline parameter values are as in Figure1.

6.description: Effects of belief dispersion on trading volume measure. These figures plot the equilibrium trading volume measureVtagainst current belief dispersionvtin Panel A and against stock volatilityσStin Panel B for different relative risk aversion coefficients γ. The baseline parameter values are as in Figure1.

7.description: Effects of belief dispersion in the two-investor economy. This figure plots the equilibrium stock priceStin Panel A, mean returnμStin Panel B, stock volatility in Panel C, and trading volume measure in Panel D against cash-flow news in an otherwise identical two-investor economy with an optimistic and a pessimistic investor. The dotted lines in Panels A and B correspond to the stock priceStpand mean returnμStpin an economy with a single pessimistic investor with a bias in beliefs of−3.39%. The dashed lines in Panels A and B correspond to the stock priceStoand mean returnμStoin an economy with a single optimistic investor with a bias in beliefs of 3.39%. The dotted lines in Panels C and D correspond to the stock volatility and trading volume measure in an economy with a single investor. The dashed lines in Panels C and D correspond to the stock volatility and trading volume measure in an economy with belief dispersion 1 standard deviation higher than the average. The other parameter values are as in Figure1.
